coredata.dc:description: Creatinine levels in blood and urine can be used as an important biomarker for the diagnosis of renal, thyroid, and muscle malfunctions. In this work, a square-wave voltammetric method for creatinine was developed using a gold electrode modified with a film composed by Nafion mixed with a graphene quantum dots dispersion prepared in the presence of Cu2+. Such Au/Nafion-GQDs-Cu electrode relies on the interactions of creatinine with Cu2+ released from the oxidation of Cu thus decreasing the characteristic Cu/Cu2+ anodic peak at +350 mV. A linear response was obtained for the analyte in the concentration range from 1.0 × 10−6 mol L−1 (0.11 mg L−1) to 4.5 × 10−4 mol L−1 (50.9 mg L−1). The proposed sensor was simple to prepare and the method was successfully applied for creatinine determination in urine samples.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Creatinine levels in blood and urine can be used as an important biomarker for the diagnosis of renal, thyroid, and muscle malfunctions. In this work, a square-wave voltammetric method for creatinine was developed using a gold electrode modified with a film composed by Nafion mixed with a graphene quantum dots dispersion prepared in the presence of Cu2+. Such Au/Nafion-GQDs-Cu electrode relies on the interactions of creatinine with Cu2+ released from the oxidation of Cu thus decreasing the characteristic Cu/Cu2+ anodic peak at +350 mV. A linear response was obtained for the analyte in the concentration range from 1.0 × 10−6 mol L−1 (0.11 mg L−1) to 4.5 × 10−4 mol L−1 (50.9 mg L−1). The proposed sensor was simple to prepare and the method was successfully applied for creatinine determination in urine samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Creatinine (2-amino-1-methyl-5-H-imidazol-4-one) is a decomposition product from the creatine phosphate in muscles [1] being excreted at a relatively constant rate, proportional to the individual's muscle mass. Creatinine is removed from circulation by glomerular filtration and excreted in the urine [1]. Levels of creatinine are used for the diagnosis of renal, thyroid, muscle malfunction [2], biomedical diagnosis of acute heart attack, and the quantitative evaluation of the hemodialysis therapy [3]. The normal concentration of creatinine in human urine is in the range from 0.5 to 1.5 mg dL−1 and from 0.7 to 1.2 mg dL−1 in blood serum [4].

As one of the most important biomarkers, it is extensively used as an internal standard in the metabolomic studies [5]. Therefore, it is crucial to develop reliable and simple analytical methods to monitor creatine in body fluids. The methods most frequently used to measure creatinine are based on the Jaffe's reaction, based on the reaction with picric acid in an alkaline solution to form a red-orange chromogen. This method is subject to positive interference by a large number of compounds, such as acetone and glucose. Alternatively, the determination can be made based on the color developed when creatinine reacts with 3,5-dinitrobenzenoic acid or its derivatives, being successfully adapted as a test strip diagnosis. However, the color produced is less stable than that of the Jaffee chromogen [1].

In order to increase selectivity in the analysis of food and biological samples, several methods based on liquid chromatography have been introduced. Zuo et al. used hydrophilic interaction liquid chromatography with photometric detection (absorbance at 205 nm) to determine creatinine in milk and orange juice. The limit of detection (LOD) was 0.39 μmol L−1 [6]. Yokoyama et al. presented a high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) method, using a weak-acid cation-exchange column, for the determination of creatinine in urine samples (LOD = 0.34 μmol L−1 using absorption photometric detection and linear range up to 500 μmol L−1) [7]. Fraselle et al. used high-resolution HPLC-tandem mass spectrometry to determine creatinine in urine (LOD of 44.2 μmol L−1) [8]. Ponhong et al. employed flow injection analysis to determine creatinine in urine from 5.3 to 884 μmol L−1 [9]. Using a portable microfluidic system, Songjaroen et al. determined creatinine in urine (LOD of 29.2 μmol L−1) [10]. However, all of the methods mentioned require time-consuming sample preparation and a summary of other reported analytical methods for creatinine is in Table S1 [11–31].

In order to increase selectivity in the analysis of food and biological samples, several methods based on liquid chromatography have been introduced. Zuo et al. used hydrophilic interaction liquid chromatography with photometric detection (absorbance at 205 nm) to determine creatinine in milk and orange juice. The limit of detection (LOD) was 0.39 μmol L−1 [6]. Yokoyama et al. presented a high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) method, using a weak-acid cation-exchange column, for the determination of creatinine in urine samples (LOD = 0.34 μmol L−1 using absorption photometric detection and linear range up to 500 μmol L−1) [7]. Fraselle et al. used high-resolution HPLC-tandem mass spectrometry to determine creatinine in urine (LOD of 44.2 μmol L−1) [8]. Ponhong et al. employed flow injection analysis to determine creatinine in urine from 5.3 to 884 μmol L−1 [9]. Using a portable microfluidic system, Songjaroen et al. determined creatinine in urine (LOD of 29.2 μmol L−1) [10]. However, all of the methods mentioned require time-consuming sample preparation and a summary of other reported analytical methods for creatinine is in Table S1 [11–31].

Electroanalytical techniques have also been used for creatinine determination [32]. Yadav et al. proposed a biosensor for creatinine using a mixture of three enzymes: amidohydrolase, creatine amidinohydrolase, and sarcosine oxidase. These enzymes were covalently immobilized onto carboxylated multi-walled carbon nanotube/polyaniline nanocomposite film electrodeposited over the surface of a Pt electrode. The LOD was 0.1 μmol L−1 with analytical response up to 750 μmol L−1 using Tris-HCl buffer (pH 4.7) and amperometric measurement at +200 mV [33].

Several types of electrode-based non-enzymatic creatinine sensors are reported in literature using copper as a means to indirectly determine creatinine. Han et al. employed cyclic voltammetry (CV) to determine creatinine in urine samples using poly(ethyleneimine)/phosphotungstic acid multi-layer modified electrode with Cu2+ added into the supporting electrolyte solution (NH4-HCl buffer, pH 5.4). Creatinine was determined by measuring the redox peak current of Cu(II)-creatinine complex/Cu(I)-creatinine complex. Linear response from 0.125 to 62.5 μmol L−1 [34]. Recently, Gao et al. developed an electrochemical sensor for creatinine-based on graphene oxide modified with copper nanoparticles. The hydrothermal reaction of graphene oxide with dopamine hydrochloride was conducted to prepare reduced graphene oxide (rGO), and self-polymerization of polydopamine (PDA) attaching on rGO. Nile blue (NB) was loaded on rGO under ultrasonication. The resulting PDA rGO-NB complex was deposited on the surface of the glassy carbon electrode under cyclic voltammetry scanning to electrodeposit copper nanoparticles, through electrochemical reduction of Cu2+, onto the PDA-rGO-NB surface. Linear concentration range was from 0.01 to 100 μmol L−1 [35]. Characteristics of other electroanalytical methods reported for creatinine are summarized in Table S2 [36–46].

Several types of electrode-based non-enzymatic creatinine sensors are reported in literature using copper as a means to indirectly determine creatinine. Han et al. employed cyclic voltammetry (CV) to determine creatinine in urine samples using poly(ethyleneimine)/phosphotungstic acid multi-layer modified electrode with Cu2+ added into the supporting electrolyte solution (NH4-HCl buffer, pH 5.4). Creatinine was determined by measuring the redox peak current of Cu(II)-creatinine complex/Cu(I)-creatinine complex. Linear response from 0.125 to 62.5 μmol L−1 [34]. Recently, Gao et al. developed an electrochemical sensor for creatinine-based on graphene oxide modified with copper nanoparticles. The hydrothermal reaction of graphene oxide with dopamine hydrochloride was conducted to prepare reduced graphene oxide (rGO), and self-polymerization of polydopamine (PDA) attaching on rGO. Nile blue (NB) was loaded on rGO under ultrasonication. The resulting PDA rGO-NB complex was deposited on the surface of the glassy carbon electrode under cyclic voltammetry scanning to electrodeposit copper nanoparticles, through electrochemical reduction of Cu2+, onto the PDA-rGO-NB surface. Linear concentration range was from 0.01 to 100 μmol L−1 [35]. Characteristics of other electroanalytical methods reported for creatinine are summarized in Table S2 [36–46].

In this work, a square-wave voltammetric method was developed for the determination of creatinine using a gold electrode modified with a film (ink) of Nafion containing graphene quantum dots produced by hydro-exfoliation of a molten of citric acid and Cu(NO3)2 (Nafion-GQDs-Cu). GQDs are nanometric fragments of graphene, where electron transport is confined in their dimensions. As GQDs are carbon-based nanostructures, they are bio-compatible and present low toxicity. It is also possible to modify them during or after production. This new sensor was simple to prepare and the method was used to determine creatinine, based on the formation of a copper-creatinine complex during Cu/Cu2+ oxidation on the surface of the electrode, enabling detection capability for the analysis of urine samples.

Voltammetric experiments were made using a potentiostat μ-AUTOLAB Type III (Metrohm, The Netherlands). A three-electrode system was used with a working gold electrode modified with a Nafion dispersion containing GQDs-Cu, a saturated Ag|AgCl(KCl(sat)) reference electrode, and an auxiliary electrode (Pt wire). The working cell, made of Pyrex glass (15 mL), was adapted to a Teflon cap with hole-positions to place electrodes and purging gas tubing. Total carbon measurements were made on a Carbon Analyzer model TOC-VCPN (Shimadzu, Japan). Photoluminescence measurements were made on an LS 55 luminescence spectrometer (Perkin-Elmer) (600 nm min−1 scan rate, 10.0 nm spectral bandpass, and 1 cm optical path length quartz cuvettes). Chromatographic analyzes were performed on an Agilent 1200 series HPLC system (Agilent, Japan) equipped with a G1315C model diode array type absorption photometric detector and an automatic sampler.

Images from the GQDs and GQDs-Cu were made using a field emission scanning electron microscope (JEOL, model JSM-7800F, Japan) operated in the scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM) mode at 30 kV. Raman spectroscopy was performed using a confocal Raman microscope (WITec Alpha 300R, Germany) equipped with an electron-multiplying charge-coupled device detector and an air-cooled solid-state laser. Raman measurements were obtained using a 532 nm laser source, a monochromator with a 600 lines/mm grating and 1 mW of laser radiation power. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) and zeta-potential measurements have been made on a nanoparticle analyzer model SZ-100 (Horiba, Japan). Zeta potential measurements were made using an acrylic electrochemical cell containing a flat carbon electrode (6 mm thickness), and DLS measurements were obtained using glass cuvettes with 1 cm optical path length. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy analysis was performed in an ultrahigh vacuum (~10−9 mbar) using a surface analysis chamber equipped with a VG Thermo Alpha 110 hemispherical analyzer and using non-monocromatized Al-Kα line as the X-ray source. The pHmeter was a model mPA 210 from Tecnopon (Brazil) with a glass membrane electrode conjugated with an Ag|AgCl(KClsat) reference electrode. The ultrasonic bath (9 L) was an NSC 2800 model from Unique, Brazil.

Ultrapure water (resistivity <18 MΩ cm) was obtained from a water purifier Milli-Q Gradient System A10, Millipore (USA). Nafion solution (5% v/v) and creatinine were obtained from Sigma Aldrich (USA). Sodium hydroxide (98.0% m/m), acetic acid (65% m/v), boric acid (99.8% m/m), phosphoric acid (85%, m/v), potassium chloride, acetate sodium, methanol (HPLC-grade), and citric acid were obtained from Merck (Germany). Aluminum oxide suspension (1.0 μm) was obtained from Fortel (Brazil). Nitrogen gas (99.999% purity) was from Messer gases (Brazil). PTFE syringe filters (0.45 μm) were from Whatman (UK). The dialysis membrane (retained molecular weight of 3.5 kDa) was from Spectrum Laboratories Inc. (USA). Britton-Robinson (BR) buffer stock solutions were prepared at 0.04 mol L−1 by mixing acetic acid, boric acid, and phosphoric acid, then adjusting to pH 4.0 by the addition of aliquots of NaOH solution (1 mol L−1). The stock KCl solution was 0.5 mol L−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of graphene quantum dots modified with cu(NO3)2
ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots modified with Cu(NO3)2 (GQDs-Cu) were produced by the pyrolysis of citric acid with Cu(NO3)2·3H2O. First, 0.6 g of citric acid was placed into a 5 mL beaker and heated to about 240 °C using a heating plate. As citric acid became molten (with color changing from colorless to pale yellow and then to light brown) within 2 to 5 min, 0.09 g Cu(NO3)2·3H2O was added to the molten and mixed using a glass stick. After about 4 min, the hot melted mixture (dark-brown molten) was added into 20 mL of ultrapure water, at room temperature to obtain (after filtering on a 0.22 μm syringe filter) a clear pale aqueous dispersion.

The synthesis of Cu(OH)2 was carried out by adding, dropwise at constant stirring, a NaOH 1 mol L−1 solution to a Cu(NO3)2 solution (at 0.1 mol L−1) until pH 10.0 is obtained. The precipitate was filtered and washed with ethanol and water to remove impurities.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Modification of the gold electrode with GQDs-Cu
ce:section.ce:para: The gold electrode surface was polished with 1.0 μm aluminum oxide suspension, then with 1.0 μm diamond polish slurry, finally washing with ultra-pure water prior to modification. The GQDs-Cu aqueous original dispersion (20 mL) was evaporated until about 2 mL, then an aliquot of 100 μL was mixed with 100 μL of a Nafion solution (5% m/v), and the mixture was vortex agitated. After ultrasonic agitation, for 25 min, 1 μL of this dispersion was placed onto the gold electrode and dried using an infrared lamp (5 min).

Cyclic voltammograms were made with a scanning velocity from 10 to 900 mV s−1 in the ±1000 mV potential range. The supporting electrolyte consisted of a mixture of the Britton-Robinson (BR) buffer solution (final concentration in the cell of 0.02 mol L−1 within the pH range from 3.0 to 7.0) and KCl solution (final concentration in the cell of 0.25 mol L−1). Square-wave voltammetry (SWV) was employed to study the redox process and to perform quantitative determination of creatinine using BR buffer solution (pH 4.0; 0.02 mol L−1 and KCl 0.25 mol L−1). Quantification was performed by measuring peak height, at the potential E
P = +415 mV, using anodic scanning from −1000 to +1000 mV at 20 Hz using 10 mV step potential and 20 mV pulse amplitude.

A volume of 60 mL of fresh human urine was treated to reduce protein content by adding 10 mL of methanol/acetonitrile (50/50% v/v). After vortex mixing (5 min) and centrifugation (15 min at 1000 RFC), the sample supernatant was collected.

Analyzes by HPLC were made based on literature [47] using isocratic elution of a mobile phase consisting of a mixture of water/acetate buffer (pH 4.2; 0.1 mol L−1) 60/40% v/v. The chromatographic column (4.6 × 250 mm and 5 μm particle size) was an Eclipse XDB-C18 from Agilent. Chromatography was performed at a 0.8 mL min−1 and 25 °C, with an introduced sample volume of 20 μL. The retention time of creatinine was 2.4 min and results were based on three replicates with absorciometric detection at 220 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of GQDs and GQDs-Cu in aqueous dispersion
ce:section.ce:para: The GQDs and the GQDs-Cu showed photoluminescence emission with maximum respectively at 445 nm and 452 nm, under excitation at 357 nm (Fig. 1A). The photoluminescence of GQDs is about 3 times more intense than the one of GQDs-Cu. Therefore, it seems that photoluminescence intensity of GQDs was quenched in the presence of copper, probably due to either the coordination to carboxyl groups on the edges and defects of GQDs or by direct reduction of Cu2+ to Cu, in this way removing electrons from the conductance band and affecting excitonic recombination [48]. The redshift of 7 nm also indicates decreasing in energy bandgap. The Raman spectra of the GQDs and GQDs-Cu are shown in Fig. 1B. GQDs spectrum exhibited typical G and 2D-bands at 1349 cm−1 and 1607 cm−1, respectively. Characteristic Raman features (D and G bands) indicated sp2 carbon in graphitic nanostructures. In contrast, the Raman spectra of GQD-Cu presented three Raman peaks at 272, 322, and 607 cm−1. Three Raman active modes can be considered, assigning the peak at 272 cm−1 to the Ag and the peaks at 322 and 607 cm−1 to the Bg modes. These wavenumbers are close to those reported in the literature (288, 330, and 621 cm−1) [49,50]. The total carbon content in the original aqueous GQDs dispersion, prepared by hydro-exfoliation of 0.5 g of citric acid, was about 143 mg L−1.

Since the nanomaterials original aqueous dispersions present themselves as viscous samples, dilution with water (3:1 water/original nanomaterial dispersion, v/v) was made to obtain STEM images of GQDs (Fig. 1C) and GQDs-Cu (Fig. 1D). It was observed an average diameter of 35.9 ± 2.2 nm for GQDs and 27.6 ± 4.2 nm for GQDs-Cu, also confirmed by dynamic light scattering (DLS) that indicated hydrodynamic sizes of 34.0 ± 3.4 nm (for GQDs in Fig. 1E) and 24.0 ± 3.1 (for GQDs-Cu in Fig. 1F). The slightly higher hydrodynamic size for GQDs than for GQDs-Cu is in agreement with the results reported by Teymourinia et al., and it is attributed to the charge of copper atoms hindering more effectively the aggregation of nanomaterial [51].

The surface of the GQDs is negatively charged due to the presence of carboxylic acid groups. At the range of pH 3.0–5.0, the negative surface of the GQDs is efficiently neutralized by H+ ions and ζ-potential values above −4 mV were obtained (inserted in Fig. 1E). As the pH was increased to 6.0, the surface became more negative due to the lower availability of H+ ions. For the aqueous dispersion of GQDs-Cu, at pH 3.0 and 4.0, ζ-potential values about −9 mV were obtained, and as pH was increased to 5.0 and 6.0, a ζ-potential value about −15 mV was measured due to the lower availability of H+ ions. These results are in agreement with the literature [52] and showed that a higher negative surface charge in the GQDs-Cu was obtained (insert in Fig. 1F) compared to GQDs. These results also explain the high stability that copper confers on GQDs, minimizing the aggregation of nanoparticles.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) indicated the presence of copper in the GQDs structure. In Fig. 2A has presented the survey spectra and the peaks for carbon 1 s (~285 eV) and oxygen 1 s (~535 eV) regions. The survey spectra showed the presence of copper (0.3%), oxygen (30.7%), nitrogen (1.3%), and carbon (67.7%) in the sample. No other elements were found at the limit of spectral resolution. The spectrum showed C 1 s peaks at 284.8 ± 0.1 eV and 288.7 ± 0.1 eV (Fig. 2B), which can be assigned to the contributions of CC sp2 and sp3, and the CO respectively. Spectra also shows Cu 2p peak (Fig. 2C) at binding energy 935 and 955 eV. Cu (I) oxide and Cu metal profiles are similar [53]. Therefore, the presence of copper(I) oxide or Cu metal or a mixture of both can be considered. Those XPS spectral features strongly indicated the functionalization of GQDs with copper.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cyclic voltammograms of creatinine using a gold electrode modified with Nafion-GQDs-Cu
ce:section.ce:para: Initially, different electrodes as glassy carbon (GC), gold, and platinum were used to evaluate the redox properties of creatinine at 2.0 × 10−4 mol L−1 (using different supporting electrolytes such as ammonium chloride; pH 4.5 and Britton Robinson buffer; pH 3.0 to 7.0) by performing cyclic voltammograms within ±1500 mV. In all cases, no electrochemical activity was observed. According to the literature, creatinine appears not to present significant electrochemical activity, which means that the direct transfer of electrons between creatinine and electrode is not favorable [34]. In fact, most studies reported in literature propose the electrochemical determination of creatinine using a mediator such as enzymes [33,35,45,54,55], copper in solution, or as a modifier on the surface of electrodes [34,45,56]. When using copper, the determination of creatinine is based on the chelation property of the analyte rather than its redox behavior [34], which means that creatinine is probed as it affects the redox response of copper. Taking advantage of this, the modification of GQDs using Cu(NO3)2 seemed to be adequate as a sensor for creatinine. Therefore, different electrode substrates (GC, Au, and Pt) were modified (deposition of the GQDs-Cu dispersion, dried at room temperature). Based on results from four independent replicates, only Au provided consistent stability to the deposited film and enable electrochemical response in terms of the redox signal concerning copper.

In order to produce the improved mechanical stability for the GQDs-Cu on the surface of the Au electrode and a reliable reproducible response, Nafion was mixed to the dispersion of GQDs-Cu prior to deposition. As an inert polymeric film, Nafion provides a mechanical barrier that minimizes the leaching of the modifier material from the electrode surface. Thus, Nafion was mixed GQDs-Cu dispersion in a 1 to 1 volume proportion, and this Nafion-GQDs-Cu mixture was used as the electrode modifier.

Cyclic voltammograms using the Au electrode modified with the Nafion-GQDs-Cu were obtained in the presence and in the absence of creatinine in the measured solution (Fig. 3 ). The CV obtained for the supporting electrolyte (BR buffer 0.02 mol L−1; pH 4 in KCl 0.25 mol L−1) without creatinine presented one anodic peak (peak 1) with peak potential at E
p = +350 mV and two cathodic peaks, as the scan was reversed, with E
p = +824 mV (peak 2) and E
p = +632 mV (peak 3).

The anodic peak (peak 1) is attributed to Cu/Cu2+ oxidation process, and the cathodic ones are probable due to the reduction of Cu2+ to Cu+ (peak 2) and the reduction of Cu+ to Cu (peak 3). This experimental observation is similar to the previously reported by Shaikh et al. when studying the reduction of Cu2+ in Britton-Robinson (BR) aqueous buffer (at various pH values) using GC electrode [57]. Peak 2, due to the Cu2+/Cu+ electron transfer couple, is less intense than peak 3 concerning the Cu+/Cu reduction couple. This can be explained considering the relative stability of Cu2+ and Cu+ ions, which depends highly on the solvent. Stability under aqueous conditions is significantly affected by the hydration energy of the ions coordinated with the water. The Cu2+ ion possesses a greater charge density than Cu+, hence, forming much stronger coordination [57]. In contrast, Cu+ is significantly stable in non-aqueous media [58] and with ligands such as Cl− [59]. In non-aqueous solvents, a more effectively solvating of Cu+ occurs, comparing to Cu2+, leading to a relative higher stability of Cu+ [57].

In the presence of creatinine, a decrease in current of peak 1 and a shifting toward more positive potentials (to E
p = +363 mV) were observed, whereas the currents measured from both cathodic peaks increased with a more significant increase for peak 3. It can be proposed that during the anodic scan, Cu is oxidized to Cu2+ and the Cu2+-creatinine complex forms on the surface of the electrode, as it is reported to occur in solution [60]. Thus, the decrease in anodic peak current can be attributed to the formation of such a complex. In the reverse scan, the formation of the creatinine complex with Cu+ seems to be hindered as sequential additions of creatinine in the electrochemical cell produces no further significant changes in peak current (Fig. S1). In polar solvents, Cu2+ complexes are predominantly coordinated to five or six ligands. However, Cu+ complexes are expected to favor four (tetrahedral) or lower coordination numbers. Therefore, electron transfer involving Cu2+ and Cu+ would be accompanied by major structural and stereochemical changes [57]. In addition, the reduction of Cu+ to Cu implies losing all ligands. For this reason, in the reverse scan, there was no significant change in peak intensities as there is no complexation taking place.

In the presence of creatinine, a decrease in current of peak 1 and a shifting toward more positive potentials (to E
p = +363 mV) were observed, whereas the currents measured from both cathodic peaks increased with a more significant increase for peak 3. It can be proposed that during the anodic scan, Cu is oxidized to Cu2+ and the Cu2+-creatinine complex forms on the surface of the electrode, as it is reported to occur in solution [60]. Thus, the decrease in anodic peak current can be attributed to the formation of such a complex. In the reverse scan, the formation of the creatinine complex with Cu+ seems to be hindered as sequential additions of creatinine in the electrochemical cell produces no further significant changes in peak current (Fig. S1). In polar solvents, Cu2+ complexes are predominantly coordinated to five or six ligands. However, Cu+ complexes are expected to favor four (tetrahedral) or lower coordination numbers. Therefore, electron transfer involving Cu2+ and Cu+ would be accompanied by major structural and stereochemical changes [57]. In addition, the reduction of Cu+ to Cu implies losing all ligands. For this reason, in the reverse scan, there was no significant change in peak intensities as there is no complexation taking place.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of Nafion and graphene quantum dots on the electrode surface
ce:section.ce:para:
The role of GQDs in the GQDs-Cu nanocomposite used as a modifier of the Au electrode was evaluated by performing a comparative experiment using Au electrode modified only with a mixture of Cu(OH)2 (in 1 μL Nafion dispersion onto the surface of the electrode and dried under the infrared lamp). Then, increased concentrations of creatinine were added into the electrochemical cell to be monitored using the Au electrode modified Cu(OH)2. Results obtained by cyclic voltammetry showed a new anodic peak (at −20.9 mV) besides the reported one (E
p = +424.9 mV), and the two cathodic peaks in the reverse scan (Fig. 4A). The anodic peaks probably correspond to the species Cu+ and Cu2+ from Cu(OH)2 and CuOH formed during the Cu(OH)2 synthesis, respectively. The Cu2+ peak potential obtained using the Au Nafion-GQDs-Cu electrode was about 75 mV smaller than the potential observed for Cu2+ using the Au electrode modified with Nafion-Cu(OH)2, demonstrating that GQDs facilitate electro-oxidation of copper. When increasing creatinine concentrations added into the cell, to be monitored by the Au electrode modified with Nafion-Cu(OH)2, no changes in current, after the first addition of creatinine, was observed (Fig. 4A). Results showed that the participation of GQDs is relevant, mediating oxidation of Cu and the electron transfer, also increasing the active area that favors the formation of the creatinine-Cu2+-complex.

Regarding the electrochemical behavior of creatinine on the Au/Nafion-GQDs-Cu electrode, the relationship between scan rate and anodic peak current (E
p = +350 mV) can be used to obtain valuable information. A sequence of cyclic voltammograms of creatinine (at 4.0 × 10−5 mol L−1) was recorded using the Au/Nafion-GQDs-Cu electrode, in BR buffer (pH 4.0; 0.02 mol L−1 in KCl 0.25 mol L−1), at different scan rates (from 10 to 800 mVs−1) as seen in Fig. 5A. An increase in I
p was found to be directly proportional to the increase of scan rate, with a determination coefficient (R2) of 0.990 (Fig. 5B), which reveals an adsorption-controlled process.

The choice of the pH to perform the voltammetric measurements were made based on the intensity of the measured Cu/Cu2+ oxidation peak and its behavior in the presence of creatinine. Square-wave voltammetry (SWV) was chosen to perform quantitative measurements using a mixture of BR buffer (at 0.02 mol L−1) and KCl (at 0.25 mol L−1) and anodic scanning within ±1000 mV (Fig. 6 ).

The creatine-creatinine equilibrium was reported to be essentially independent upon temperature, between 22 and 40 °C, but to be strongly dependent on pH [61]. Literature suggests that at lower pH (pH < 2), creatine, in a creatine-creatinine mixture, will be ultimately converted to creatinine [61]. At pH > 6.0, approximately equimolar ratio of creatine and creatinine will be established, but at pH 3.5–4.0, the formation of creatinine is favored (> 90%). Therefore, a SWV study was performed in the pH range from 3.0 to 7.0, comparing the intensities obtained for the peak at E
p = +415 mV. The maximum anodic peak current was achieved at pH 3.0–5.0 (Fig. 6A). Changing the pH of the electrolyte was found to affect peak position (shift to less positive potentials as pH increases). As pH values increased from 4.0 to 7.0, both E
p values of the redox process and I
p decreases probably due to influence of creatine and also to the formation of Cu(OH)2 on the surface of the electrode which is indicated by the enlargement of the voltammetric peaks toward less positive potentials. At pH 3.0 and 4.0, a sharp voltammetric peak is obtained and better relative responses were found as creatinine was added to the electrochemical cell (Fig. 6B-F). The supporting electrolyte at pH 4.0 was selected for further studies.

The amount of the Nafion-GQDs-Cu to be added onto the surface of the Au electrode was also evaluated. Different amounts of the modifier (from 1 μL to 4 μL) were added onto the electrode surface and used to evaluate their response toward creatinine added into the electrochemical cell. The best condition was chosen based on the ratio between the signal of blank (Ip0) and the signal measured in the presence of creatinine (Ip(cre)) at 1.0 × 10−5 mol L−1. The best response (lower Ip0/Ip(cre) ratio) was found using a deposition of 1 μL of the modifier (Fig. 7 ) as the lower Ip0/Ip(cre) means that electrode saturation would be achieved at higher concentrations of creatinine. The use of the lower amount of modifier also benefited the drying process, making it faster under the infrared lamp and enabling better mechanical stability of the modification onto the Au electrode surface.

The stability of the Au/Nafion-Au-GQDs-Cu electrode was evaluated by obtaining 20 repetitive cyclic voltammograms using 4.0 × 10−5 mol L−1 of creatinine. The measured average peak currents for creatinine were 16.4 μA with the relative standard deviation of 1.8%.

A study was performed aiming to achieve conditions appropriated to determine creatinine using the Au/Nafion-GQDs-Cu electrode by SWV. The supporting electrolyte was the BR buffer (pH 4.0; 0.02 mol L−1) and KCl (0.25 mol L−1), and signal intensity was measured at E
p = +415 mV. Pulse amplitude steadily increased the voltammetric peak measured without creatinine as it was varied (from 10 to 100 mV) with 20 mV selected due to the better relationship between peak sharpness and intensity. Frequency (f) of 20 Hz was a compromise between peak current and peak width within the 5 to 100 Hz range, and the potential step chosen was 10 mV. These studies were made in the presence of creatinine.

It is considered that on applying potential, copper undergoes electrochemical oxidation and the formed Cu2+ combines with the creatinine at the electrode surface, forming the Cu2+-creatinine complex. The formation of the complex decreases the concentration of free Cu2+ formed and released by the electrode surface, thus decreasing the characteristic Cu/Cu2+ current and leading to an analytical response inversely proportional to the increasing concentration of creatinine in the electrochemical cell. The normalized analytical curve was constructed by sequential additions of analyte into the electrochemical cell (Fig. 8A and Fig. 8B), based on the I
p0
/I
p(cre) ratio, where, I
p0 is the peak current of the blank and I
p(cre) is the peak current measured in the presence of creatinine. There are two linear ranges relating measured peak current and creatinine concentration: range 1 (Fig. 8B curve a, from 5.0 × 10−8 to 1.0 × 10−6 mol L−1) and range 2 (Fig. 8B curve b from 1.0 × 10−6 to 4.5 × 10−4 mol L−1). However, the range covering the higher concentration levels was chosen for quantitative purposes as it is enough to guarantee the determination of typical creatinine levels in urine samples. In such range of concentrations, the response is modeled by the equation I
p0
/I
p(cre) = (6.56 ± 0.16) × 103 C
creatinine + (1.48 ± 0.03) with R2 of 0.990. The C
creatinine is the analyte concentration in mol L−1. The instrumental limit of quantification (LOQ) was considered as the lower concentration in this range that still keeps the linear response at least at R2 = 0.990. Pearson's correlation coefficient (ρ) was calculated to test the linear response of this instrumental LOQ. A ρ value of 0.993 indicated a strong correlation and linearity for 1.0 × 10−6 mol L−1 (0.11 mg L−1).

The instrumental precision was obtained as the variation coefficient of 10 consecutive measurements of the signal produced by the analyte concentrations at 1.0 × 10−5 mol L−1 and at 1.2 × 10−4 mol L−1. Each measurement was made after solution agitation to replenish the solution-electrode interface. The precision at 1.0 × 10−5 mol L−1 was 2.7% while at 1.2 × 10−4 mol M-1 was 1.9%.

Accuracy of the method was evaluated by determining two different concentration levels of creatinine in control solutions (fortifications at 5.0 × 10−5 mol L−1 and at 5.0 × 10−6 mol L−1). The recoveries obtained (n = 3) were respectively (4.6 ± 0,1) × 10−5 mol L−1 (93 ± 3.1%) and (4.4 ± 0.2) × 10−6 mol L−1 (89 ± 4.2%) showing the potential of the Au/Nafion-GQDs-Cu electrode as an electrochemical sensor for the detection of creatinine.

Studies were conducted to evaluate the possible interferences imposed by some of the components of urine on the electroanalytical creatinine signal measured using the Au/Nafion-GQDs-Cu electrode. Following information for healthy individuals [1], normal creatinine values in urine vary from 0.6 to 1.3 mg dL−1 (5.3 × 10−4 to 1.1 × 10−3 mol L−1). For the potential interferents, uric acid values vary from 2.6 to 7.2 mg dL−1 (1.5 × 10−3 to 4.2 × 10−3 mol L−1), urea values vary from 7 to 18 mg dL−1 (1.1 × 10−2 to 2.9 × 10−2 mol L−1) while glucose vary from 0.02 to 0.2 mg dL−1 (8 × 10−4 to 8 × 10−3 mol L−1).

In the experiment, creatinine concentration in the electrochemical cell was kept at 5.0 × 10−6 mol L−1 while the concentration of each of the potential interferents in the solution of mixed potential interferents was adjusted in two different concentrations: urea (2.0 × 10−4 mol L−1 and 4.0 × 10−4 mol L−1), uric acid (2.5 × 10−5 mol L−1 and 5.0 × 10−5 mol L−1), glucose (5.0 × 10−5 mol L−1 and 1.0 × 10−4 mol L−1). Potassium ions and sulfate ions (both at 5.0 × 10−6 mol L−1 or 1.0 × 10−5 mol L−1) were also tested.

The addition of 5.0 × 10−6 mol L−1 of creatinine (without interferents) in the electrochemical cell-induced a value to Ip0/Ipcre of 1.10. There was an increase in the measured signal of less than 5%, due to the presence of the mixture of potential interferents. Hence, most of the major substances that compose the urine matrix imposed a negligible influence on the variation of the signal produced by creatinine. These results have indicated a good selectivity of the Au/Nafion-GQDs-Cu electrode toward creatinine.

Three fresh urine samples were analyzed to demonstrate the applicability of the SWV method based on the use of the Au/Nafion-GQDs-Cu electrode. Urine samples were provided from three healthy adult volunteers, including two females and one male. One of the female volunteers and the male volunteer were under a diet rich in animal protein while the diet of the other female volunteer mostly comprised of vegetables and low amounts of animal protein.

A volume of 60 mL of urine sample was submitted to a procedure to remove most of the protein content and insoluble residues by adding 10 mL of methanol/acetonitrile (50/50% v/v) mixture. Urine samples were fortified with creatinine at three levels: 10 μmol L−1 (1.1 mg L−1), 150 μmol L−1 (16.9 mg L−1), 300 μmol L−1 (33.9 mg L−1). The average analyte original contents, calculated by the average recovery achieved using the fortification values, are indicated in Table 1 . The values obtained from the analyte fortification procedure enabled estimation of the original contents of creatinine that agreed with the original values quantified in samples using HPLC. The results are presented in Table 2 . The Student t-test for similar variances (previously verified using the Fisher-Snedecor test) was used to compare the mean values of the results of each method. The statistical test considered a confidence limit of 95% (two tails, each with α/2 = 0.025). The test indicated that results are in statistical agreement (texperimental < tcritical). The result can be considered satisfactory, considering the complexity of the matrix.

Different works have been dedicated to the electroanalytical determination of creatinine [3,32–34,54,56]. However, many require laborious procedures for electrode modification, as well as time-consuming sample treatment or the use of expensive reagents. In this work, a simple pyrolysis reaction of citric acid with a small amount of Cu(NO3)2 was used to produce a nanocomposite, which was used to create a non-enzymatic electrochemical sensor of creatinine. A simple electrode modification procedure provided a sensing for creatinine in urine after only a simple sample pre-treatment, covering a concentration range enough to allow determination of typical concentrations of creatinine in urine. Reproducible results and proper recoveries besides LOD, which correspond to the same or lower order of magnitude of the best ones reported in the literature (Table S2), are features that indicate advantages of the proposed sensor.

Different works have been dedicated to the electroanalytical determination of creatinine [3,32–34,54,56]. However, many require laborious procedures for electrode modification, as well as time-consuming sample treatment or the use of expensive reagents. In this work, a simple pyrolysis reaction of citric acid with a small amount of Cu(NO3)2 was used to produce a nanocomposite, which was used to create a non-enzymatic electrochemical sensor of creatinine. A simple electrode modification procedure provided a sensing for creatinine in urine after only a simple sample pre-treatment, covering a concentration range enough to allow determination of typical concentrations of creatinine in urine. Reproducible results and proper recoveries besides LOD, which correspond to the same or lower order of magnitude of the best ones reported in the literature (Table S2), are features that indicate advantages of the proposed sensor.
